questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum primary circuit breaker setting for a transformer with impedance greater than 6% but not more than 10%, installed at any location?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a transformer with impedance greater than 6% but not more than 10% at any location, the maximum primary circuit breaker setting is reduced to 400% of the rated primary current. Compare this to 600% for transformers with impedance not more than 6%.
sparkyTip: Higher impedance = lower primary CB setting. At ≤6% you get 600%, but above 6% it drops to 400%. Higher impedance transformers limit fault current less on the secondary, so tighter primary protection is needed.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum primary fuse rating for a supervised location with primary-only protection?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a supervised location with primary-only protection (no secondary OCPD), the maximum primary fuse rating is 250% of the rated primary current. This is lower than the 300% permitted when secondary protection is also provided, because the primary device must provide all the transformer protection by itself.
sparkyTip: Supervised primary-only: fuse = 250%, breaker = 300%. These are tighter than the "any location" values because there is no secondary backup device.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum secondary fuse rating for a transformer with impedance not more than 6%?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a transformer with impedance not more than 6%, the maximum secondary fuse rating is 250% of the rated secondary current. The secondary circuit breaker is permitted up to 300%. Remember, fuses always get a lower percentage than circuit breakers.
sparkyTip: Secondary protection follows the same fuse-vs-breaker pattern: fuse at 250%, breaker at 300% for ≤6% impedance. For >6%, it drops to 225% fuse / 250% breaker.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), how does increasing transformer impedance from ≤6% to greater than 6% affect the maximum primary circuit breaker setting at any location?
options: It remains at 600% regardless of impedance
explanation: When transformer impedance increases above 6%, the maximum primary circuit breaker setting decreases from 600% to 400% of rated primary current. Higher impedance means the transformer provides less fault current limiting on the secondary side, so tighter primary overcurrent protection is required to compensate.
sparkyTip: Think of it this way: low impedance = transformer limits faults well = looser primary protection (600%). High impedance = less fault limiting = tighter primary protection (400%).

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum secondary circuit breaker setting for a transformer with impedance greater than 6% but not more than 10%?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a transformer with impedance greater than 6% but not more than 10%, the maximum secondary circuit breaker setting is 250% of the rated secondary current. This is reduced from 300% for transformers with impedance not more than 6%.
sparkyTip: Higher impedance tightens ALL protection settings: Primary CB drops 600%→400%, Secondary CB drops 300%→250%, Secondary fuse drops 250%→225%. Only the primary fuse stays at 300%.
